firstAid: Call emergency services (112 or 102) immediately.
Have the person sit down, rest, and try to stay calm.
Loosen any tight clothing.
If the person is fully conscious and has been cleared for aspirin, have them chew and swallow an aspirin (325mg).
If the person is unconscious and not breathing, begin CPR immediately if trained.
whenToVisitDoctor: This is a life-threatening medical emergency. Seek immediate emergency care.
prevention: Regular exercise
Heart-healthy low-sodium diet
Manage stress
Avoid smoking
Control blood pressure and blood sugar
description: A heart attack occurs when the flow of blood to the heart muscle is severely reduced or blocked, usually due to a buildup of fat, cholesterol and other substances.

causes: Blocked artery in the brain (ischemic stroke)
Ruptured blood vessel in the brain (hemorrhagic stroke)
firstAid: Use the B.E. F.A.S.T. protocol to evaluate signs.
Call 112/102 emergency ambulance immediately.
Note the exact time symptoms first appeared; this is crucial for treatment.
Do NOT give the person any food, drinks, or medications (especially aspirin, as it can worsen bleeding strokes).
Keep them lying down comfortably on their side if they are breathing.
whenToVisitDoctor: Immediate emergency medical attention is required. Every minute counts.
prevention: Maintain blood pressure below 120/80
Stop smoking
Control cholesterol
Regular cardio exercise
Healthy balanced diet
description: A stroke occurs when the blood supply to part of your brain is interrupted or reduced, preventing brain tissue from getting oxygen and nutrients.

causes: Dengue virus transmitted by female Aedes aegypti mosquitoes
riskFactors: Living in or traveling to tropical/subtropical climates
firstAid: Rest comfortably under a mosquito net to prevent spreading.
Give Paracetamol (Crocin/Dolo 650) to manage fever and pain.
Do NOT give Aspirin, Ibuprofen, or Diclofenac, as these increase bleeding risks.
Hydrate continuously with ORS, coconut water, and fresh juices.
whenToVisitDoctor: Visit a doctor if fever exceeds 102°F or is accompanied by severe eye/joint pain. Blood test for platelet count is essential.
prevention: Eliminate standing water
Use insect repellents (DEET)
Wear long-sleeved shirts and full pants
Use mosquito nets and window screens
description: Dengue is a mosquito-borne viral disease that causes severe flu-like symptoms and can progress to severe dengue (dengue hemorrhagic fever) which can be fatal.

emergencySigns: Inability to speak in full sentences
firstAid: Help the person sit upright.
Help them use their rescue inhaler (Albuterol / Levosalbutamol) - typically 2-6 puffs.
Keep them calm; anxiety can worsen airway constriction.
If there is no improvement within 15 minutes or symptoms are critical, call 112 immediately.
whenToVisitDoctor: If you experience frequent shortness of breath, wheezing attacks, or cough that worsens at night.
prevention: Identify and avoid asthma triggers (pollen, dust, smoke)
Monitor lung function with a peak flow meter
Get annual flu vaccinations
description: Asthma is a chronic condition where your airways narrow, swell, and produce extra mucus, making breathing difficult and triggering coughing and wheezing.

firstAid: If conscious and blood sugar is low (hypoglycemia, <70 mg/dL), administer 15g of fast-acting glucose (fruit juice, sugar water, honey).
Wait 15 minutes and check blood sugar again. Repeat if still low.
If unconscious, seek immediate emergency help and do NOT put liquids in their mouth.
whenToVisitDoctor: Consult a doctor if you notice persistent fatigue, frequent urination, or excessive thirst for a routine HbA1c screening.
prevention: Eat a diet rich in vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins
Engage in 150 minutes of moderate exercise weekly
Maintain a healthy weight
description: Type 2 diabetes is a chronic metabolic condition characterized by high blood glucose levels caused by the body resisting insulin or not making enough of it.

riskFactors: Consuming spicy, fatty, or acidic foods
firstAid: Stand or sit upright.
Drink a small glass of warm water or cold milk to soothe the esophagus.
Take an over-the-counter antacid like Digene syrup or a proton pump inhibitor if prescribed.
Loosen tight clothing around your waist.
whenToVisitDoctor: Visit a doctor if you have symptoms more than twice a week, or if OTC medications do not provide relief.
prevention: Eat smaller, more frequent meals
Do not lie down within 3 hours of eating
Elevate the head of your bed
Avoid fatty, fried, or spicy foods
description: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) is a chronic digestive disease in which stomach acid or bile flows back into your food pipe (esophagus), irritating its lining.

firstAid: Get plenty of physical rest.
Inhale steam with a drop of eucalyptus oil.
Gargle with warm salt water to relieve a sore throat.
Stay hydrated by drinking warm water, herbal teas, or chicken soup.
whenToVisitDoctor: If symptoms last more than 10 days, or if you develop severe sinus pain, wheezing, or difficulty breathing.
prevention: Wash hands frequently with soap and water
Avoid close contact with infected individuals
Maintain respiratory hygiene
description: The common cold is a viral infection of your nose and throat (upper respiratory tract). It is usually harmless, though it may not feel that way.

causes: Salmonella typhi bacteria ingested through contaminated food or water
firstAid: Rest in bed comfortably.
Hydrate vigorously with boiled and cooled water with ORS.
Apply cool wet compresses on the forehead to reduce high temperatures.
Seek medical advice immediately for an antibiotic prescription.
whenToVisitDoctor: If you have a high fever that does not go down with paracetamol, accompanied by severe stomach aches. Requires Widal test/blood culture.
prevention: Drink boiled, bottled, or purified water
Wash hands thoroughly before eating
Avoid raw, unpeeled fruits and vegetables from street vendors
Get vaccinated against typhoid
description: Typhoid fever is a life-threatening systemic bacterial infection contracted by ingesting food or water contaminated with the feces of an infected person.

symptoms: Darkening of skin in creases (acanthosis nigricans)
riskFactors: Family history of PCOS or Type 2 Diabetes
emergencySigns: Sudden severe pelvic pain (indicates ruptured ovarian cyst)
Heavy vaginal bleeding leading to dizziness
firstAid: Keep a warm heating pad on the lower abdomen during severe menstrual cramps.
Ensure a high-fiber, low-glycemic meal to manage blood sugar spikes.
Sip spearmint herbal tea to help reduce androgen symptoms naturally.
whenToVisitDoctor: See a gynecologist if you have missed multiple periods, are struggling to conceive, or notice excessive new facial hair growth.
prevention: Maintain a balanced low-sugar diet
Incorporate strength training and regular cardio
Maintain a healthy body mass index (BMI)
description: PCOS is a common hormonal disorder among women of reproductive age, characterized by irregular menstrual cycles, excess male hormones, and ovaries containing multiple small fluid-filled follicles.

causes: Abnormal brain activity affecting nerve signals, chemicals, and blood vessels in the brain
emergencySigns: Headache with fever, stiff neck, or mental confusion (signs of meningitis)
firstAid: Rest in a quiet, dark, and cool room.
Apply a cold compress or ice pack to your forehead or the back of your neck.
Drink plenty of water to rule out dehydration.
Take prescribed abortive medications or an OTC painkiller early in the attack.
whenToVisitDoctor: If your headaches are frequent, severe, disrupt daily activities, or if you experience a headache that feels completely different from past episodes.
prevention: Maintain a regular sleep schedule
Keep a headache diary to identify triggers
Manage stress through yoga or meditation
Avoid skipping meals
description: A migraine is a neurological condition that causes intense, debilitating headaches, often accompanied by sensory disturbances and stomach upset.

symptoms: Passing frequent, small amounts of urine
Red, bright pink or cola-colored urine
causes: Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria entering the urinary tract through the urethra
emergencySigns: Severe pain in the back or side (kidney infection)
firstAid: Drink plenty of water to help flush out the bacteria.
Avoid drinks that can irritate your bladder, such as coffee, alcohol, and soft drinks.
Use a heating pad on your abdomen to ease pelvic discomfort.
Consult a doctor for an antibiotic course; do not ignore UTIs.
whenToVisitDoctor: As soon as you notice pain or burning during urination, frequent urging, or cloudy urine, to get an antibiotic prescription.
prevention: Drink plenty of liquids, especially water
Wipe from front to back after urinating or a bowel movement
Empty your bladder soon after intercourse
Avoid irritating feminine products
description: A urinary tract infection (UTI) is an infection in any part of your urinary system — your kidneys, ureters, bladder and urethra. Most infections involve the lower urinary tract.

symptoms: Small, raised bumps that may leak fluid
riskFactors: Personal or family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever
Exposure to harsh soaps or allergens
emergencySigns: Skin looks infected (pus, red streaks, yellow crusts)
Fever or feeling generally unwell with severe flare-up
firstAid: Apply a thick, fragrance-free moisturizer or cold-pressed coconut oil.
Avoid scratching the itchy skin; trim fingernails short or wear cotton gloves.
Take lukewarm baths rather than hot showers and use mild, non-soap cleansers.
Apply an OTC hydrocortisone cream on highly inflamed patches.
whenToVisitDoctor: If the skin rash interferes with sleep or daily activities, if it is painful, or if you suspect a bacterial skin infection.
prevention: Moisturize skin at least twice a day
Identify and avoid food/environmental triggers
Use a humidifier in dry air
Wear soft, breathable cotton clothing
description: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that causes dry, red, and extremely itchy patches, most common in children but can occur at any age.
